content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Over the past year, I’ve noticed something changing.
People are building.
Not large enterprise systems.
Not necessarily products.
Just… software.
Someone prototypes an internal tool over the weekend.
A team uses AI to automate a workflow that used to take hours.
A department builds a chatbot to answer common questions.
A proof of concept quietly becomes something people rely on every day.
What started as an experiment slowly becomes part of how the business operates.
I don’t say any of that as criticism. Quite the opposite.
I think it’s one of the most exciting shifts our industry has seen.
For as long as I’ve been building software, one of the biggest constraints wasn’t ideas.
It was our ability to turn those ideas into reality.
AI is changing that.
It’s reducing the effort required to move from an idea to working software faster than anything I’ve seen in my career.
But one observation keeps coming back to me.
AI hasn’t removed complexity.
I think it’s moved it.
For years, one of the biggest questions technology organizations asked was:
Can we build it?
Increasingly, I think the more important question is becoming:
Who owns it after we do?

Yesterday’s Bottleneck
For much of my career, engineering capacity acted as a natural filter.
Organizations almost always had more ideas than they had people available to build them.
The path usually looked something like this:
Ideas → Funding → Engineering Capacity → Software
Engineering teams became the constraint.
That wasn’t always enjoyable, but it forced prioritization.
Not every good idea became software.
Today, AI is changing that equation.
Increasingly, the path looks more like this:
Ideas → AI → Software
That doesn’t mean software builds itself.
Nor does it diminish the role of engineers.
It simply means one of the biggest historical constraints is shrinking.
Creating software is becoming dramatically easier.
Every time one constraint disappears, another becomes more important.

Today’s Bottleneck
For years, organizations primarily asked:
Can we build it?
Increasingly, I think the better question is:
Can we sustainably own it?
Because software doesn’t stop existing after it’s deployed.
Someone still has to:

• Secure it

• Monitor it

• Support it

• Patch it

• Upgrade dependencies

• Respond when it fails

• Document it

• Answer audit questions

• Pay for the infrastructure

• Plan enhancements

• Eventually replace it
AI can help us create software.
It doesn’t eliminate the long-term responsibility that comes with choosing to own it.
One of the more interesting shifts AI is creating is that it changes what naturally filters ideas.
Historically, engineering capacity forced difficult conversations.
There simply wasn’t enough time or enough people to build everything.
As AI reduces the cost of building, “Can we build it?” is becoming a less useful filter.
Instead, I think the more important strategic question becomes:
Should we own it?
Because every application we choose to create is also a long-term commitment.
A commitment to:

• Maintain it

• Support it

• Secure it

• Govern it

• Integrate it

• Improve it

• Eventually retire it
The technical barrier to creating software is getting smaller.
The strategic decision to own software is becoming much larger.

The Hidden Cost of “Just One More Tool”
One pattern I’ve started noticing is that AI makes every individual solution feel inexpensive.
A chatbot.
A workflow.
An internal dashboard.
An AI agent.
A small automation.
Viewed individually, none of them seem particularly significant.
Organizations don’t own individual applications.
They own portfolios.
Every new solution introduces another authentication flow.
Another deployment pipeline.
Another integration.
Another monitoring requirement.
Another dependency.
Another security review.
Another application someone has to understand after its original creator has moved on.
One application rarely creates an operational burden.
Fifty applications create an ecosystem.
Whether you intended to build one or not.

Learning to Read the Whole Field
Long before I worked in technology, I played football.
As a safety, my responsibility wasn’t simply making tackles. It was understanding how one change affected everyone else on the field.
Over the years, I’ve realized that’s also how I think about enterprise technology.
Very few architectural decisions exist in isolation.
Every application changes security.
Every integration changes operations.
Every automation changes ownership somewhere else.
AI hasn’t changed that.
It’s simply moved where the pressure shows up.

Tomorrow’s Competitive Advantage
I think AI is quietly changing what technology leadership looks like.
Historically, much of our energy went into enabling creation.
Removing blockers.
Increasing engineering capacity.
Helping teams deliver faster.
Those things still matter.
But as AI lowers the effort required to create software, I think leadership increasingly becomes an exercise in stewardship.
Not because innovation should slow down.
Because innovation deserves to last.
The conversations begin to change.
Who owns this?
How does it integrate with everything else?
Does it align with our engineering standards?
What happens when the original creator leaves?
Who supports it during an outage?
Can we explain it to an auditor?
Will we still want to own it five years from now?
Those aren’t obstacles to innovation.
They’re the questions that allow innovation to become sustainable.

This Isn’t an Argument Against AI
None of this is an argument against AI.
Quite the opposite.
I believe AI will be one of the most transformative forces our industry has ever experienced.
The opportunity is enormous.
That’s exactly why I think these conversations matter.
History suggests that every technology that dramatically lowers the cost of creation eventually forces organizations to become more disciplined about ownership.
Cloud computing didn’t eliminate governance.
It changed where governance mattered.
APIs didn’t eliminate architecture.
They made architecture more important.
Data democratization didn’t eliminate stewardship.
It made stewardship essential.
I don’t think AI will be any different.
AI is reducing one of the greatest constraints software has ever had: engineering capacity.
That doesn’t eliminate constraints.
It changes where they exist.
I think the organizations that thrive over the next decade won’t simply be the ones that build the fastest.
They’ll be the ones that recognize where complexity moved — and prepare for it.
Because if today’s bottleneck is sustainable ownership…
Then the next question becomes obvious.
What kind of architecture makes enterprise AI sustainable?
That’s the conversation I’d like to explore next.
